sublevels.title: C++ CLASSES
sublevels.subtitle: OOP in C++ — constructors, access, methods

teaching.title: C++ Classes vs Structs
teaching.analogy: C++ class is a C struct with superpowers — methods, access control, constructors, destructors, operator overloading. The only difference between class and struct in C++ is the DEFAULT access: struct is public, class is private.
teaching.content: class: members are private by default. struct: members are public by default. Both support all OOP features in C++. Convention: use class for OOP entities with invariants, struct for plain data (like C structs).

examples.title: C++ Class
Struct vs Class in C++

teaching.funFact: C++ class and struct are identical in capability — only default access differs. Bjarne Stroustrup actually prefers struct for simple data types. The C++ Core Guidelines say: use struct when all data is public and the type has no invariants! 📐

teaching.title: Member Initializer List & const Methods
teaching.analogy: The member initializer list is the C++ way to initialize members AT construction time — more efficient than assigning in the body. const methods are a promise: 'I won't modify any member variables.' The compiler enforces this contract.
teaching.content: Initializer list: Constructor(params) : member1(val), member2(val) { }. Preferred over body assignment — required for const and reference members. const method: can't modify members — const correctness.

examples.title: Initializer List
examples.explanation: Initialize members before constructor body runs.

teaching.funFact: const methods and the const qualifier are C++'s formal system for expressing immutability contracts. The compiler enforces them at compile time. This is why C++ is used in safety-critical systems — errors caught at compile time, not runtime! ✅

mcq.q: C++ class vs struct — ONLY difference?
options: class members are private by default; struct members are public by default
mcq.explanation: Default Access: That's the ONLY language-level difference. class = private by default. struct = public by default. Otherwise identical in C++.

mcq.q: Initializer list: Engine(int c):cylinders(c){} — what does it do?
options: Initializes cylinders BEFORE the body runs — more efficient, required for const members
mcq.explanation: Before Body: Initializer list runs before the constructor body. For const members and reference members, it's the ONLY way to initialize them. Preferred for all members.

mcq.q: A const method in C++ means:
options: Method cannot modify any non-mutable member variables of the object
mcq.explanation: No Modification: const after the parameter list (void show() const) means the method won't modify the object's state. Enables calling on const objects.
Class Only: private members are ONLY accessible within the class body itself — not from derived classes, not from outside. Use protected for subclass access.

mcq.q: Can a const member variable be initialized in the constructor BODY?
options: No — must use initializer list: Class(int n):constMember(n){}
mcq.explanation: Must Use List: const members must be initialized at construction — before the body runs. Initializer list is the only way. Assignment in the body is too late — the const was already default-initialized.

coding.title: Initializer list syntax
coding.objective: Constructor that initializes int x and string name via init list:
coding.hint: Initializer list starts with a colon after the parameter list.

coding.title: const method
coding.objective: Getter that doesn't modify object — add keyword:
coding.hint: const after params = method won't modify members.

coding.title: Default access class vs struct
coding.starterCode: struct Point { int x; int y; }; // x and y are ___ by default
coding.hint: struct default = public. class default = private.

options: const member can't be assigned in constructor body — use initializer list: Box(int s):size(s){}
debugging.answer: const member can't be assigned in constructor body — use initializer list: Box(int s):size(s){}
debugging.hint: const members must be initialized in the initializer list, not assigned in the body. The body runs after construction — too late for const.

prediction.explanation: n initialized to 0 via init list. inc() twice: n=2. get() returns 2.

teaching.title: Destructor & RAII
teaching.analogy: The destructor is C++'s automatic cleanup crew — when an object goes out of scope, the destructor runs automatically. RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) is the principle: acquire resources in constructor, release in destructor. No manual cleanup needed!
teaching.content: Destructor: ~ClassName() — runs when object destroyed (scope ends or delete called). RAII: resources (memory, files, mutexes) tied to object lifetime. Smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr) implement RAII for memory.

examples.title: Destructor & RAII
Smart Pointers (RAII for memory)

teaching.funFact: RAII is considered C++'s most important idiom. Bjarne Stroustrup said RAII is what makes C++ safe despite manual memory management. It's why STL containers like vector never leak — their destructors free memory automatically! 🛡️

mcq.q: When does a C++ destructor run?
options: Automatically when object goes out of scope or delete is called
mcq.explanation: Automatic: Destructor runs automatically when: (1) local object goes out of scope, (2) delete is called on heap object. No manual call needed — that's the power of RAII!

options: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization — tie resource lifetime to object lifetime
mcq.explanation: RAII: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization. Acquire in constructor, release in destructor. When object dies, resource is automatically released. No leaks if used correctly.

mcq.q: unique_ptr<int> p = make_unique<int>(5). When is the int freed?
options: When p goes out of scope — unique_ptr destructor deletes it
mcq.explanation: Automatic: unique_ptr's destructor calls delete on the managed pointer. When p goes out of scope, the int is freed. No explicit delete needed — no leaks!
~ClassName(): Destructor has tilde prefix, same name as class, NO return type, NO parameters. Called automatically — cannot be overloaded.

coding.title: Destructor syntax
coding.objective: Write destructor declaration for class Game:

coding.title: smart pointer
coding.objective: Create unique_ptr to int with value 42:
coding.hint: make_unique<T>(value) — T is the type being managed.

options: Memory leak — new[] must be matched with delete[]. Use unique_ptr<int[]> data=make_unique<int[]>(100); for RAII.
data goes out of scope so it's freed
debugging.answer: Memory leak — new[] must be matched with delete[]. Use unique_ptr<int[]> data=make_unique<int[]>(100); for RAII.
debugging.hint: new int[100] allocates on heap. Scope exit doesn't free heap. Use RAII (unique_ptr) or explicitly delete[].

prediction.explanation: Constructor: 'ON '. Body: 'WORK '. Destructor on scope exit: 'OFF'.

sublevels.title: OPERATOR OVERLOADING
sublevels.subtitle: Making operators work with custom types

teaching.title: Operator Overloading in C++
teaching.analogy: Operator overloading teaches C++ the meaning of +, -, *, ==, << for your types. Just as int knows what 5+3 means, your Vector class can know what vec1+vec2 means. It's teaching the compiler new vocabulary for your types.
teaching.content: Define operator functions: Type operator+(const Type& rhs) const. Common: +,-,*,/,==,!=,<,<=,>>,<<. << for cout output. == for comparison. Can be member or friend function. Don't overload ., ::, ?:, sizeof.

examples.title: Operator Overloading

teaching.funFact: C++ std::string uses operator+ for concatenation, operator<< for cout, operator== for comparison, and operator[] for character access. Every string operation you've used is an overloaded operator! 🔤

mcq.q: Vec2 operator+(const Vec2& o) const — return type is:
mcq.explanation: Vec2: The + operator for Vec2 should return a NEW Vec2 (the sum). Must match what the operation produces logically.

mcq.q: friend ostream& operator<<(ostream& os,const T& t) — why friend?
options: Allows access to private members from outside the class (ostream is not T)
friend is required for all operators
mcq.explanation: Private Access: operator<< has ostream as left operand (not T). As a free function, it needs access to private members. 'friend' grants that access from outside the class.

mcq.q: Can you overload the . (dot member access) operator in C++?
options: No — C++ explicitly forbids overloading . :: ?: and sizeof
mcq.explanation: Forbidden: The operators that cannot be overloaded: . (dot), :: (scope), ?: (ternary), sizeof, typeid, .* (pointer-to-member). All others can be overloaded.

coding.title: Operator overload keyword
coding.objective: Declare operator+ for adding two Box objects:
coding.hint: C++ syntax: returnType operator+(const Type& rhs) const

coding.title: Equality check
coding.objective: Point a(1,2),b(1,2). After defining operator==: a==b is?
coding.starterCode:
// operator==(const Point& o) const {return x==o.x&&y==o.y;}
// Point a(1,2),b(1,2); a==b is: ___
coding.hint: Both points have same x,y. operator== returns true.

options: operator+ takes non-const Money& but 'a' is const. Fix: operator+(const Money& other) const
debugging.answer: operator+ takes non-const Money& but 'a' is const. Fix: operator+(const Money& other) const
debugging.hint: operator+ should be const-correct: (const Money& other) const. Allows calling on const objects.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE C++ RUNTIME
teaching.analogy: The C++ Runtime constructs and destructs every object, manages every resource, and resolves every operator. It knows RAII, const methods, initializer lists, and operator overloading in its sleep. Defeat it by mastering C++'s object model.
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 6 C++: class vs struct default access, initializer list, const methods, destructor ~ClassName(), RAII principle, unique_ptr, operator overloading syntax, friend operator<<.

examples.title: Full C++ OOP Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ class = private default; struct = public
✅ Ctor(params):member1(v),member2(v){}
✅ const method — no member changes
✅ const member — must use init list
✅ ~Class() — auto-runs on scope exit
✅ RAII: resources in ctor/dtor
✅ unique_ptr — RAII memory
✅ operator+(const T&) const
✅ friend ostream& operator<<

teaching.funFact: C++ is the only major language where the programmer has FULL control over object lifetime — constructors, destructors, copy/move semantics, RAII — all user-defined. This is why game engines, VR, and finance systems use C++! 🎮

mcq.q: class A has no user-defined destructor. When local A object goes out of scope:
options: Compiler generates default destructor — runs automatically
mcq.explanation: Auto-Generated: Compiler provides a default destructor that calls member destructors. For classes with raw pointers, define your own to delete them properly.

mcq.q: unique_ptr<int> p=make_unique<int>(5); After scope exit, memory is:
mcq.explanation: Auto-Freed: unique_ptr owns the pointee. Its destructor calls delete on scope exit. No explicit delete needed. This IS RAII.

mcq.q: Vec2 operator+(const Vec2& o) const — trailing const means:
options: THIS object's members won't be modified — callable on const Vec2
mcq.explanation: Method const: Trailing const = 'this' method doesn't modify *this. Required to call operator+ on const Vec2 objects.

mcq.q: struct vs class in C++ — which is correct?
options: struct members are public by default; class members are private by default
struct is C, class is C++
mcq.explanation: Default Access Only: The ONLY difference. Both fully support methods, constructors, inheritance, templates in C++.

mcq.q: RAII pattern: when should resources be released?
options: In destructor — automatically when object goes out of scope
mcq.explanation: Destructor: RAII releases resources in the destructor. Since destructors run automatically on scope exit, resources are ALWAYS released — even if exceptions occur!

coding.title: Boss: Full class init list
coding.objective: class Tank{const int armor; int hp; Tank(int a,int h):___,hp(h){}}. Fill blank:
coding.hint: Must initialize const member 'armor' in init list: armor(a).

coding.title: Boss: Smart pointer
coding.hint: make_unique<string>('hello') creates a heap string managed by unique_ptr.

options: Memory leak — no ~Resource() to delete data. Add: ~Resource(){delete[] data;}
data goes out of scope with r
debugging.answer: Memory leak — no ~Resource() to delete data. Add: ~Resource(){delete[] data;}
debugging.hint: C++ has no GC. Heap memory allocated in constructor MUST be freed in destructor — this is RAII pattern!

prediction.explanation: r1 ctor: A. r2 ctor: A. Body: M. r2 dtor (LIFO): Z. r1 dtor: Z. Output: AAMZZ.
